name: Aoraki Mackenzie
note: One of the largest gold-tier dark sky reserves on Earth, ringed by the Southern Alps. The Southern Cross circles the pole here all year, and the winter air off the glaciers is optically superb.

name: NamibRand
note: Africa's first dark sky reserve; the desert absorbs what little light remains. The nearest permanent lamp is beyond the horizon in every direction — a claim almost nowhere else can make.

name: Atacama
note: The driest desert on Earth and, by many measures, the clearest sky. Three hundred cloudless nights a year, and air so stable the professionals put their largest mirrors here.

name: La Palma
note: Protected by law since 1988 — the world's first sky legislation. Above the trade-wind cloud, the air over the Roque is treated as what it is: a scientific instrument.

name: Lofoten
note: Deep inside the auroral oval, where winter nights are nearly whole and the lights stand overhead rather than on the horizon. The sea keeps the cold honest and the air clean.

name: Kerry
note: A gold-tier reserve on the Atlantic edge of Europe, rare for its latitude. Between weather fronts the sky is startling — and the weather fronts are part of the education.
